# Chapter 1 Summary : Overdeliver

![](https://cdn.bookey.app/files/pdf/book/en/images/a61a118955306612998c8ed9318c3e3631e2d7782408c3815b2052fc5ec07eb6.jpg)

# SUMMARY OF CHAPTER 1: OVERDELIVER

# Introduction

- The essence of successful business and life revolves around relationships, not merely revenue.

- The author reflects on their extensive experience in direct response marketing and emphasizes the value of continuous self-improvement and overdelivering.


# The Four Pillars of Being Extraordinary

- To achieve greatness in marketing and life, one must

embrace the following four pillars:

# 1\. Outwork Everyone

- Success is a product of hard work and relentless commitment to improvement.

- Celebrate your achievements but strive to exceed them by beating existing benchmarks.


# 2\. Possess Insatiable Curiosity

- Cultivating curiosity broadens understanding and insight into diverse subjects, enhancing your marketing approach and personal growth.

# 3\. Surround Yourself with Smart People

- Learning from those with greater knowledge accelerates personal and professional development.

# 4\. Help Others First

- A generous attitude fosters strong relationships and business success. Focus on how you can benefit others.

# The Control is Your Enemy

- Always seek to surpass current performance standards in marketing. The best marketers continually aim to improve their work, even after achieving significant success.

# The Power of 100:0

- Relationships thrive when one party commits fully without expecting equal effort in return, promoting genuine connections.

# Givers, Takers, and Matchers

- People fall into one of these categories based on their approach to relationships. Givers, while at risk of being taken advantage of, ultimately succeed by creating meaningful connections.

# Key Takeaways

- Overdelivering and fostering strong relationships through effort and integrity are critical to sustainable success.

- Embrace the mindset of abundance and generosity, aiming to provide exceptional value to customers and colleagues alike.


# Conclusion

- Achieving balance in life and work through a commitment to excellence and a focus on relationships embodies the philosophy of overdelivery.

# Example

Key Point:The importance of relationships over revenue in achieving success

Example:Imagine you're at a networking event, and instead of just handing out your business card, you take time to listen to others. You genuinely engage in conversations, seeking to help where you can. By prioritizing the relationship over the sale, you establish a deeper connection. Later, when someone remembers your kindness, they refer others to you. This interaction exemplifies the principle that prioritizing relationships leads to greater long-term success than merely chasing profits.

# Critical Thinking

Key Point:The emphasis on relationships over revenue highlights a fundamental tension in business values.

Critical Interpretation:While Kurtz argues that prioritizing relationships leads to success, it's important to consider that this view may not universally apply across all industries. For example, a purely transactional approach might deliver better results in highly competitive or price-sensitive markets. This indicates that the author's perspective, though valuable, may not be the only path to success. Scholars such as Thomas Sowell in 'Basic Economics' and Clayton Christensen in 'The Innovator's Dilemma' suggest that market dynamics can also dictate success through efficiency and innovation rather than solely through relationship building.

# Chapter 2 Summary : Original Source

![](https://cdn.bookey.app/files/pdf/book/en/images/861527c6f076c99c25e097b6eb9faae412cdf5b5bbf946914713d8850f64d9c7.jpg)

# CHAPTER 2 SUMMARY

# Introduction to Original Source Material

The importance of mastering the fundamentals in marketing is emphasized through a quote often attributed to Pablo Picasso, highlighting the need to learn rules thoroughly to innovate effectively. The author credits his success to the foundational principles developed by legendary marketers, noting that understanding these elements can foster creativity and long-term business success.

# Core Principle \#1: Marketing is About Psychology

Understanding the psychology of your market is crucial. This encapsulates knowing customer desires and respecting their intelligence. The author references Gene Schwartz’s belief that marketers should not create demand but rather channel existing desires. Maintaining a connection with the market's pulse is vital for ongoing success.

# Core Principle \#2: There’s Nothing Wrong with Shortcuts

Utilizing the lessons learned by previous marketers is not laziness; it is an intelligent strategy to avoid costly mistakes. Acknowledge that successful marketing has been built on knowledge, and shortcutting through historical insights allows for a more effective approach to current marketing strategies.

# Core Principle \#3: Use What’s Already Working

Successful marketing often involves leveraging existing ideas. The author illustrates this through examples of effective marketing copy that originated from unrelated contexts. Observing and adapting winning ideas from various sources can greatly enhance marketing effectiveness.

# Core Principle \#4: Swipe Files Are Your Secret Weapon

Swipe files, which compile successful marketing examples, are invaluable tools for marketers. Studying proven copy and campaigns can inspire new ideas and streamline learning, allowing marketers to benefit from the successes of others without starting from scratch.

# Core Principle \#5: That New Thing Might Not Be So New

Recognizing that many modern techniques stem from foundational principles can help marketers avoid arrogance and improve their strategies. By studying past techniques, marketers can gain insights into currently popular methods and enhance their effectiveness.

# Key Takeaways

- Mastering marketing principles enables innovation and the ability to break rules creatively.

- The five key principles of direct marketing emphasize psychology, the value of shortcuts, utilizing proven ideas, the indispensability of swipe files, and the relevance of historical techniques.

- Developing a deep understanding of these principles can lead to sustained business success and meaningful customer relationships.


# Chapter 3 Summary : How Paying Postage Made Me a Better Marketer

CHAPTER 3 SUMMARY: HOW PAYING POSTAGE MADE ME A BETTER MARKETER

Brian Kurtz shares his extensive experience in direct mail marketing, emphasizing the valuable lessons learned from managing significant postage costs throughout his career. Despite the rise of online marketing, he argues that the lessons from direct mail are essential, regardless of whether marketers plan to use this method.

# The Importance of Postage in Marketing

Kurtz reflects on the risks and expenses associated with direct mail, highlighting how the steep costs of postage forced marketers to be meticulous in their campaign strategies. He compares this to the more casual approach many modern online marketers adopt, noting the tendency toward less precision in list selection and campaign execution due to lower costs.

# Being Precise and Avoiding Sloppiness

The author emphasizes the need for careful audience analysis and segmentation, advocating the use of RFM (recency, frequency, monetary) metrics for effective segmentation. He stresses that understanding your audience's purchasing behavior is crucial for maintaining customer relationships and driving profitability.

# Tracking Metrics and Lifelong Value (LTV)

Kurtz talks about the significance of customer acquisition costs and lifetime value in assessing marketing effectiveness. He shares insights on predicting renewals and long-term profits, urging marketers to focus on maintaining existing customers rather than solely acquiring new ones.

# Chapter 4 Summary : List Building & RFM

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| Section | Summary |
| Importance of Lists | Connecting with the right audience through quality lists is essential for marketing success. |
| 40/40/20 Rule inMarketing | Campaign success is derived from 40% list quality, 40% offer relevance, and 20% creativity(modified breakdown: 41/39/20). |
| Effective List Selection | A well-targeted list leads to success, even with average offers and creative approaches. |
| Classic Approach to ListBuilding | Marty's newsletter with a bill-me-later model showcased successful targeted list usage and qualitycontent. |
| Adapting List Strategies | Focus on aligning marketing offers with audience behaviors and expectations rather than relyingsolely on platforms. |
| Types of Lists | Response lists are superior to compiled lists due to their relevance and demonstrated interest. |
| RFM Analysis | RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) analysis segments lists based on individual interactionsand value. |
| Implementing RFM | Marketing communications should be tailored based on RFM analysis to engage high-valuecustomers. |
| Choosing the RightMarket | Focus on niche markets and leverage audience feedback for better engagement and productofferings. |
| Key Takeaways | Lists are valuable assets; prioritize response lists and utilize RFM analysis for effectivemarketing. |
| Conclusion | Applying list building and RFM principles can enhance marketing strategies and foster audienceengagement. |

# LIST BUILDING & RFM

# Importance of Lists

- The essence of marketing lies in connecting with the right

audience through well-curated lists, which are essentially real people, not mere data points.

- The author emphasizes that success is largely determined by the quality of audience lists.

# 40/40/20 Rule in Marketing

- The formula indicates that $4 0 %$ of a campaign's success is determined by the list quality, $4 0 %$ by the offer's relevance, and $2 0 %$ by the creative aspect.

- The author prefers a modified breakdown of 41/39/20, highlighting the list's priority.


# Effective List Selection

- A targeted and well-selected list can lead to success, even with average creative and offers.

- The importance of selecting a suitable and responsive list cannot be overstated.


# Classic Approach to List Building

- Marty, a key figure in the narrative, launched a newsletter using a bill-me-later offer model, allowing quicker growth by

utilizing a targeted mailing list and quality content, leading to significant success.

# Adapting List Strategies

- Reinforcement of adapting marketing offers to audience behaviors rather than being solely reliant on technology or platforms.

- Recommends focusing on quality content and match offers accordingly with audience expectations.


# Types of Lists

- Distinction between compiled lists (based on demographic data) and response lists (based on previous engagements and actions).

- Response lists are considered superior due to their relevance and demonstrated interest.


# RFM Analysis

- Recency, Frequency, and Monetary (RFM) analysis helps determine the value of individuals on a list based on their recent interactions, purchase frequency, and total money

spent.

- RFM is crucial for segmenting lists to target high-value individuals effectively.

# Implementing RFM

- Emphasizes the need to tailor marketing communications based on RFM analysis, restructuring offers based on customers' purchasing behaviors.

- Suggests that contact strategies should focus on engaging frequent and recent buyers.


# Choosing the Right Market

- Advises focusing on niche markets and leveraging audience feedback for better engagement and product offerings.

# Key Takeaways

- Lists are valuable assets of real people who should be treated like family.

- The 40/40/20 rule underlines the foundation of campaign success.

- Prioritize response lists over compiled lists for effective


campaigns.

- Utilize RFM analysis to maximize marketing efficiency and customer engagement.

# CONCLUSION

- By applying these principles of list building and RFM analysis, marketers can enhance their strategies, ensuring sustained audience engagement and business growth.

# Critical Thinking

Key Point:The emphasis on list quality in marketing campaigns is paramount.

Critical Interpretation:The chapter underscores the foundational role of quality audience lists in ensuring marketing success. While Brian Kurtz argues that lists contribute to $4 0 %$ of a campaign's effectiveness, critical perspectives might argue that this quantification simplifies the complex nature of consumer engagement and market trends. Critics may highlight psychological and social factors that influence consumer behavior, suggesting that marketing effectiveness could hinge on more than just audience lists and percentages (Fader, P. S. (2012) 'Customer Centricity: Focus on the Right Customers for Strategic Advantage'). For instance, the nuances of consumer emotions and relationships with brands may play a vital role, which are not fully captured in numerical analysis like RFM. Therefore, while Kurtz stresses list quality, it's worth examining alternative views that embrace broader aspects of marketing efficacy.

# Chapter 5 Summary : Offers

CHAPTER 5 SUMMARY: OFFERS

# Importance of Offers

- The right offer is crucial in marketing; it should be so irresistible that saying "no" feels irrational.

- The synergy between list targeting and offers is emphasized.


# Niche Selection

- Selecting a niche allows marketers to excel in specific areas rather than attempting to cater to broad audiences. - Focusing on a tight niche increases relatability and effectiveness.

# Two Case Studies on Niche Approaches

John's Niche Downsize

- John initially targeted all men facing midlife crises but was advised to specialize in lawyers, enhancing relatability and promotional efficacy.

# 2 30 Million Diabetes Niche

- An opportunity with a diabetes book was seized without the typical Q testing approach due to time constraints, resulting in a highly successful launch.

# Market-Driven Offers

- Offers should be shaped by audience feedback. The transition from Boardroom Reports to Bottom Line/Business illustrates adapting to audience preferences to maintain relevance and profitability.

# Campaign Structure's Impact

- Different promotional strategies attract diverse customer segments.

- The effectiveness of long-form copy versus gimmicky


approaches is discussed, establishing long-term relationships with customers.

# Using Proven Offers

- Identifying previously successful offers or repurposing content can yield significant results without reinventing the wheel.

- Many successful campaigns stemmed from adapting existing materials rather than creating entirely new content.


# Assessing Assets for Growth

# Lists

: Evaluate existing contacts and previous customer interactions.

# Content

: Leverage previously created or unused materials.

# Promotions

: Analyze past offers for potential repurposing or improvement.

# The Power of Overdelivering

- Adding bonuses or extras significantly enhances perceived value and response rates, as shown in the Bottom Line Yearbook example.

# Statistical Significance in Testing

- Proper testing involves ensuring that results are statistically significant before scaling offers, thus minimizing risks and maximizing informed decisions.

# Pyramid Structure for Rollout

- Gradually expanding successful tests to broader audiences while ensuring each new segment shows sustained results helps mitigate risk.

# Creativity in Offers

- The creative aspect of developing offers is crucial. Offers should align with marketing messaging, and a collaborative approach between offer creation and copywriting enhances

overall effectiveness.

# Key Takeaways

- Choose niches to develop deep expertise.
- Utilize audience feedback and adapt offers.
- Test and validate offers methodically to maximize impact. - Prioritize repurposing existing successes and leveraging bonuses.

  Chapter 6 continues the discussion on the creative aspects of marketing.

# Example

Key Point:Make your offers so compelling that your audience can't resist saying 'yes'.

Example:Imagine you're launching a fitness app targeting busy professionals. Instead of a generic subscription, you create a tailored offer that includes personalized meal plans, one-on-one coaching calls, and exclusive community access. As you present this enticing package, your audience finds it not just appealing, but feels irrational to decline an opportunity that promises to transform their health and well-being while fitting perfectly into their hectic lives.

# Chapter 6 Summary : Creative & Copy

CHAPTER 6 SUMMARY: CREATIVE & COPY

Creative and copy are as crucial to marketing success as list selection and offers. Great copy enhances the impact of the right list and offer, paving the way for long-term success in direct marketing.

# SEVEN CHARACTERISTICS OF WORLD-CLASS COPYWRITERS

# Hunger

: Successful copywriters are dedicated and willing to put in the hard work and practice necessary to hone their craft. They understand the value of transforming raw ideas into compelling copy.

# 2 Curiosity

: Insatiable curiosity drives the best writers to delve deep into topics and understand their audience thoroughly, allowing them to create highly relevant copy.

# 3.

# Smarts

: The most talented copywriters surround themselves with knowledgeable individuals, ensuring constant improvement and accountability in their work.

# Passion

: An effective copywriter is deeply connected to their subject matter, which translates into engaging and impactful writing. This passion inspires them to strive for greater impact rather than just focusing on profitability.

# 5.

# Understanding Direct Marketing Principles

: The best copywriters see themselves as partners in the marketing process, understanding the importance of lists and offers to create successful campaigns.

# Humility

# Chapter 7 Summary : Multichannel Marketing

# MULTICHANNEL MARKETING

Marketing on a single channel can be a dangerous approach for businesses. This chapter emphasizes the importance of diversifying across multiple channels to ensure long-term success and effective communication with customers.

# The Dangers of Focusing on One Channel

Businesses often gravitate towards one major platform for convenience or familiarity. However, relying solely on one channel can lead to significant risks, such as sudden policy changes that could jeopardize business operations. The ability to reach multiple audiences across different platforms can enhance engagement and maximize profits.

# Quality Over Quantity

Rather than solely targeting the largest audiences, marketers should focus on smaller, more targeted lists that can yield better lifetime value (LTV) and profitability. This is especially true for high-ticket items where fewer, more affluent customers can be more beneficial than a larger volume of lower-value sales.

# Adapting Strategies Across Channels

Marketers should view themselves as direct marketers who utilize various channels instead of defining themselves by a single platform. Understanding customer preferences and reading behaviors across different platforms is crucial for effective marketing strategies.

# Key Questions to Determine Effective Channels

To find the best channels for reaching customers, consider questions about their demographics, pain points, engagement platforms, and research habits. This insightful analysis will guide businesses in choosing the right marketing channels.

# The Importance of Expert Specialists

As marketing opportunities grow, it is essential to hire

specialists in each channel instead of relying on a one-stop-shop model. This allows businesses to maximize each channel's potential while ensuring quality output.

# Measurability and Metrics

Direct response marketing hinges on measurement. Important metrics to focus on include:

# Lifetime Value (LTV)

: Understanding how much revenue a customer generates over their relationship with the business.

# Cost to Acquire a Customer

: Evaluating all costs associated with acquiring customers through different channels.

# Attribution

: Tracking each customer’s journey through various touchpoints to assess the effectiveness of different channels.

# Conversion Rates

: Tracking the behaviors of customers after their initial purchase to assess ongoing engagement and profitability.

# Combining Online and Offline Strategies

Integrating online and offline marketing strategies can lead to higher conversion rates and customer engagement. This approach acknowledges that consumers often interact with brands through various mediums.

# Key Takeaways

Multichannel marketing not only facilitates customer engagement across numerous platforms but also protects businesses from unforeseen challenges. Embracing this model allows for increased growth potential and robust marketing strategies. Always prioritize diversity in marketing channels, focus on measurable metrics, and utilize experts in specialized fields.

# Chapter 8 Summary : Customer Service & Fulfillment

CUSTOMER SERVICE & FULFILLMENT

# Introduction to Customer Service and Direct Marketing

Brian Kurtz connects his passion for baseball with lessons in direct marketing, highlighting the importance of customer service and fulfillment in maintaining customer loyalty.

# Learning from the Umpiring Experience

Kurtz shares his journey as an umpire, likening it to the role of customer service representatives. He emphasizes that while umpires are often unrecognized, their role is crucial in ensuring a positive experience for players and fans, mirroring how customer service should operate within businesses.

# The Importance of Customer Service

Customer service is critical in retaining customers. If customers feel wronged by a company, rectifying the situation promptly can increase their lifetime value significantly. Kurtz stresses that overdelivering in service is not just a nice-to-have but essential for long-term success.

# Overdelivering and the Customer Experience

He argues that many marketers accept high return rates as normal, neglecting the need for genuine customer care and follow-through after the sale. Maintaining high-quality service throughout the customer journey is essential.

# Learning from Customer Complaints

Kurtz recounts his experience fielding late-night customer calls to address complaints, emphasizing the invaluable insights garnered from direct interaction with unsatisfied customers. This practice not only aids in problem-solving but also reveals larger systemic issues within the business.

# The Role of Secret Shoppers

Hiring secret shoppers can help identify weaknesses in customer service and sales processes. Evaluating how customers feel at each stage helps businesses refine their strategies to enhance customer satisfaction.

# Handling Market Distractions

Kurtz outlines the importance of awareness in marketing campaigns, advising against launching promotions during major world events that could distract audiences. He shares historical examples of how they timed campaigns around predictable distractions to optimize response rates.

# Creating a Barrier to Switch

The chapter concludes with strategies for establishing barriers that make it hard for customers to switch to competitors. By offering exceptional and personalized experiences, businesses can foster lasting loyalty.

# Key Takeaways

1. Prioritize customer service to enhance overall satisfaction and lifetime value.

2. Overdeliver to exceed expectations in customer fulfillment.

3. Utilize customer feedback to inform improvements in service and offerings.

4. Employ secret shoppers to gain unbiased insights into customer experiences.

5. Be mindful of market contexts and distractions when planning marketing campaigns.

6. Develop strong relationships with customers to strengthen loyalty and create hurdles for competitors.


# Chapter 9 Summary : Continuity & LTV

|     |     |
| --- | --- |
| Section | Key Points |
| Introduction to LTV and RepeatBusiness | Success in direct marketing depends on repeat business and ongoing customerrelationships. |
| The Importance of Multiple Offers | Having multiple products is essential; creating a franchise of products enhances marketpresence. |
| Understanding Lifetime Value (LTV) | LTV is a crucial metric influencing direct marketing strategies; neglecting it can leadto missed opportunities. |
| Three Risks of Ignoring LTV | Premature Abandonment: Leaving channels too early.False Positives: Misjudging profitability.Misattribution of Success: Not recognizing effective strategies. |
| The Power of Direct Response | Direct response marketing relies on understanding LTV for growth forecasting andinformed decision-making. |
| The Bogey Concept | The bogey calculates acceptable short-term losses for long-term profitability. |
| Spending Philosophy | Awareness of LTV allows for greater upfront marketing spends with a focus onlong-term returns. |
| Congruence in Marketing | All marketing initiatives should align with company values and audience expectationsto enhance LTV. |
| Dealing with Incongruence: TheMoose on the Table | Identify and address potential issues harming customer trust and LTV; incongruentoffers can lead to losses. |
| Key Takeaways | LTV is central to marketing decision-making, empowering marketers to take riskswhile maintaining congruence and trust. |

# CONTINUITY & LTV

# Introduction to LTV and Repeat Business

- The survival of a direct marketing business hinges on repeat

business.

- Key insight: A product isn’t a business; a suite of products and ongoing customer relationships are essential for long-term success.

# The Importance of Multiple Offers

- Success relies on having multiple products ready when entering the market.

- Focus on creating a franchise of products rather than relying solely on the first one.


# Understanding Lifetime Value (LTV)

- LTV is a critical metric that influences every aspect of direct marketing.

- Ignoring LTV can lead to premature channel abandonment, misattribution of success, and lost opportunities.

# Chapter 10 Summary : Playing the Long Game

CHAPTER 10: PLAYING THE LONG GAME

# Introduction to Networking

Brian Kurtz expresses his dislike for the term "networking," stemming from a past experience where he was labeled a "strategic schmoozer," leading to a desire for more meaningful, genuine connections. He emphasizes shifting from mere networking to contributing first, developing synergistic partnerships instead.

# Learning from Giants

Reflecting on lessons from influential figures in direct marketing over three generations, Kurtz illustrates that true connections emerge from the intent to contribute. By emphasizing humility, curiosity, and overdelivery, he suggests that these intangibles elevate one’s marketing

# Vitamin Connect

Kurtz critiques superficial networking accelerated by social media, advocating for a more personal approach to establish deeper connections. He introduces the concept of "Vitamin Connect," highlighting the importance of face-to-face interactions and personal touches in building meaningful relationships.

# Building Relationship Capital

Kurtz outlines the significance of relationship capital—valued more than monetary assets—stating that intentional efforts in connecting with remarkable individuals yield lasting benefits. He emphasizes the importance of genuine communication over superficial engagements.

# Intentional Dinners

Kurtz shares insights into hosting intentional dinners, inspired by a mentor. He discusses planning for memorable experiences that encourage connection and shared learning

among guests. The goal is to create an environment that fosters fruitful conversations and relationships.

# Five Strategies to Invest in Relationships

# Embrace Competition as Coexistence

: Encourage others in the field, raising overall standards and fostering mutual respect.

# Avoid Legal Disputes

: Focus on collaboration over litigation to build stronger relationships.

# 3\. Recognize Observers

: Understand that people may be watching your interactions, which can lead to unexpected opportunities.

# Send Unexpected Gifts

: Cultivate relationships year-round through thoughtful gestures, like sending gifts or personalized notes instead of just seasonal cards.

# 5\. Stay Humble

: Maintain humility regardless of success, appreciating every individual’s value in your network.

# Conclusion

Kurtz concludes with the idea that building meaningful relationships not only enriches one’s personal and professional life but also fosters a community where contributions are valued. Success comes through caring for those within your professional sphere, ensuring a lasting, impactful legacy.

# Key Takeaways

- Surround yourself with remarkable individuals who uplift you.

- Shift from networking to meaningful contribution.

- Acknowledge and nurture relationship capital as a lifetime investment.

- Organize intentional gatherings to deepen connections.

- Emphasize humility and genuine interactions in all relationships.


# Example

Key Point:Embrace Long-Term Relationship Building Over Superficial Networking

Example:Imagine hosting a dinner where each guest shares not only their successes but also their challenges. As you serve a thoughtfully prepared meal, you engage in discussions that reveal shared values and aspirations. This intimate setting allows you to forge a connection far beyond a simple business card exchange, leading to mutual support and collaboration for years to come. Such intentional efforts to nurture relationships can transform your network into a community where collective growth thrives.

# Critical Thinking

Key Point:The Concept of Relationship Capital

Critical Interpretation:Kurtz emphasizes the necessity of prioritizing relationship capital over superficial networking, arguing that real connections yield greater long-term benefits. While his insights resonate, one might question the universal applicability of his approach, as not everyone may experience the same success through deep relational investments. A contrasting perspective can be drawn from Mark Granovetter's concept of 'weak ties' in his paper 'The Strength of Weak Ties,' which suggests that superficial connections can also facilitate opportunities. Thus, it's essential for readers to weigh Kurtz's viewpoint against other theories that advocate for a balance between deep and broad social connections.

# Chapter 1 \| Overdeliver\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What is the core principle that Brian Kurtz emphasizes in Chapter 1 of 'Overdeliver'?

Answer:The core principle emphasized is that everything in business (and life) is not just about revenue, but fundamentally about relationships. Success in direct marketing and life comes from building solid relationships and consistently overdelivering to customers and stakeholders.

# 2.Question

How does Kurtz define 'overdelivering' in the context of marketing?

Answer:Overdelivering in marketing means exceeding the expectations of customers by providing exceptional value through products, services, and communications. It's about playing the long game and ensuring customer satisfaction to foster loyalty and repeat business.

# 3.Question

What are the four pillars that Brian Kurtz identifies as essential for becoming extraordinary?

Answer:The four pillars are: 1) Outwork everyone – having a strong work ethic and commitment. 2) Possess insatiable curiosity – maintaining a constant desire to learn. 3) Surround yourself with smart people – collaborating and learning from those more knowledgeable. 4) Help others first – prioritizing the needs of others, which fosters goodwill and strengthens relationships.

# 4.Question

Why does Kurtz believe that the 'control' is an enemy to marketers and copywriters?

Answer:The 'control' refers to the best-performing marketing campaign or piece currently in use. Kurtz views it as an enemy because it can create complacency. The best marketers continually strive to improve upon their successes and never stop testing and innovating, even when they have a

strong campaign.

# 5.Question

What significance does Kurtz attribute to the concept of '100:0' in relationships?

Answer:The '100:0' concept emphasizes giving $1 0 0 %$ effort in relationships without expecting anything in return. This approach alleviates the stress of keeping score and fosters stronger, more genuine connections, whether in personal or professional contexts.

# 6.Question

What role does curiosity play in achieving success, according to Kurtz?

Answer:Curiosity drives individuals to explore and understand a wide range of subjects, making them more insightful and effective in their fields. It sets them apart, allowing them to connect better with others and become authorities in their areas of expertise.

# 7.Question

How does Kurtz suggest individuals approach their professional communities?

Answer:Kurtz advises choosing professional communities wisely, surrounding oneself with mentors and peers who push you to grow. Being in the right environment with skilled individuals helps cultivate excellence and accountability in your craft.

# 8.Question

What is the impact of generosity and helping others, as discussed by Kurtz?

Answer:Generosity leads to integrity and loyalty, establishing a strong foundation for lasting relationships. By focusing on helping others first, individuals naturally cultivate goodwill, which in turn enhances their reputation and success.

# 9.Question

What is Kurtz's advice regarding the relationship between effort and relationships?

Answer:Kurtz suggests that consistent, generous effort in any relationship yields the best results, regardless of the reciprocation received. He advocates for being proactive in

maintaining relationships and contributing positively, regardless of others' efforts.

# 10.Question

How does Kurtz conclude the key takeaways from Chapter 1?

Answer:Kurtz concludes that obsessing over giving more than expected—through overdelivery—sets a path for unstoppable success. He emphasizes that life is a blend of work and play, and pursuing excellence is what ultimately leads to fulfillment.

# Chapter 2 \| Original Source\| Q&A

# 1.Question

How does understanding the fundamentals of marketing benefit your business?

Answer:Understanding the fundamentals enables you to innovate and break the rules effectively, leading to higher customer retention and better results.

# 2.Question

Why should marketers study original source materials

# rather than just follow trends?

Answer:Studying original source materials allows marketers to learn timeless principles that can result in greater successes and avoid costly mistakes.

# 3.Question

What is the significance of Gene Schwartz's perspective on customer understanding?

Answer:Gene emphasized that real success comes from understanding human psychology and existing market desires, rather than trying to create demand from scratch.

# 4.Question

How can shortcuts in learning be beneficial in marketing?

Answer:Shortcuts allow marketers to leverage the hard-earned lessons of others, saving time and reducing risk while facilitating quicker, more effective results.

# 5.Question

What role do swipe files play in a marketer's development?

Answer:Swipe files collect proven campaigns and copy, offering critical insights and inspiration that accelerate

learning and improve campaign effectiveness.

# 6.Question

What does the phrase 'that new thing might not be so new' mean in the context of marketing?

Answer:This phrase suggests that many contemporary marketing ideas are rooted in principles from the past, and understanding these origins can enhance current strategies.

# 7.Question

Why is it important to respect and understand your audience, according to Brian Kurtz?

Answer:Respecting your audience fosters trust and engagement, leading to better marketing outcomes, as customers are more likely to respond positively when they feel valued.

# 8.Question

What should marketers do when they encounter a successful marketing technique?

Answer:Marketers should let the successful technique continue to run, rather than reinventing the wheel, to maximize the effectiveness of proven strategies.

# 9.Question

How can studying the successes of past marketers help in today's marketing landscape?

Answer:Learning from past marketers helps identify effective techniques and psychological insights that still resonate, providing a strong foundation for modern marketing efforts.

# 10.Question

What is the key takeaway regarding the evolution of marketing techniques?

Answer:Many established marketing techniques, created decades ago, remain relevant today and can be combined with contemporary tools for greater impact.

# Chapter 3 \| How Paying Postage Made Me a Better Marketer\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What lesson about marketing did you learn from paying a lot of postage during direct mail campaigns?

Answer:Paying high postage taught me the importance of meticulous planning and precision in marketing. With significant costs on the line, I had to ensure every campaign was thoroughly crafted, from list selection to the offer and creative execution. This kind of discipline and attention to detail is crucial, as it directly impacts the success of the campaign.

# 2.Question

Why is it important to focus on lifetime value (LTV) in marketing?

Answer:Focusing on LTV allows you to understand the long-term value of each customer, informing how much you can afford to invest in acquiring new customers. By maximizing renewals and cross-selling opportunities, businesses can ensure profitability beyond the initial sale, thus supporting sustainable growth.

# 3.Question

How does recency, frequency, and monetary (RFM) scoring aid in effective marketing?

Answer:RFM scoring helps marketers segment their audience based on their purchasing behavior—how recently they

bought, how often, and how much they spent. This allows for more targeted and effective marketing strategies, focusing on the segments that are more likely to respond and engage, thus reducing waste.

# 4.Question

What failure did the author experience with infomercials and what did he learn from it?

Answer:The author initially failed with short-form TV ads because they didn't adequately convey the value of complex products. This taught him that understanding the medium and the audience's needs is critical, and that proper adaptation of the marketing message is essential for success.

# 5.Question

What does the author suggest about treating every communication with your audience?

Answer:Every communication should be treated as an opportunity to build rapport and trust. This means ensuring messages are congruent, relevant, and reflective of high standards, rather than seen as a mere transaction.

# 6.Question

What role does discipline play in successful marketing according to the author?

Answer:Discipline is essential for maintaining high standards in every marketing effort. It ensures that campaigns are well-researched, executed strategically, and that metrics are tracked effectively to gauge performance and learning.

# 7.Question

How can anecdotal evidence shape marketing strategies?

Answer:Following anecdotal evidence can help marketers identify successful trends and adapt their strategies accordingly. It encourages learning from previous successes or failures in the market to optimize future campaigns.

# 8.Question

What are some critical mistakes marketers should avoid according to the author?

Answer:Marketers should avoid neglecting to measure key performance metrics, being overly complacent after successful campaigns, and failing to consider the long-term implications of their marketing efforts on customer

relationships.

# 9.Question

How can online marketers apply the lessons of direct mail to improve their strategies?

Answer:Online marketers can learn to use disciplined approaches, strategic content, and proper list segmentation similar to direct mail strategies to ensure they are not being sloppy and are effectively converting audiences.

# 10.Question

What advice does the author give regarding customer care in marketing?

Answer:The author emphasizes the importance of genuinely caring about the problems your products aim to solve. Authentic concern and empathy in messaging can help build stronger relationships with customers.

Why is the list considered the most important asset in marketing?

# 2.Question

# What does the 40/40/20 rule signify?

Answer:This rule asserts that $4 0 %$ of a marketing campaign's success depends on the quality of the list, $4 0 %$ on the offer being made, and $2 0 %$ on the creative execution. This underscores the primacy of targeting the right audience.

# 4.Question

What are RFM metrics, and why are they important?

Answer:RFM stands for Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value. They help in analyzing customer behavior to predict future purchasing behavior, ensuring marketers can target their communications effectively. Segmenting based on RFM allows for customized marketing strategies, improving response rates.

# 5.Question

What is the difference between compiled lists and response lists?

Answer:Compiled lists gather demographic data without direct response history, likening to generic targeting. Response lists, however, are built on actual consumer interactions and behaviors, indicating stronger potential engagement and conversion.

# 6.Question

What lesson about list building can be drawn from Marty’s approach with Boardroom Reports?

Answer:Marty's strategy of offering a one-issue trial without upfront payment (a bill-me-later offer) demonstrated that with targeted lists and quality content, one can grow faster. This shows the power of innovative approaches in reaching potential customers without the constraints of typical payment upfront models.

# 7.Question

How can congruency in marketing impact customer retention?

Answer:Consistently delivering valuable, related offers that align with what customers initially signed up for ensures trust and loyalty, enhancing long-term relationships. Inconsistent messaging can lead to customer churn and high return rates.

# 8.Question

What is the importance of market selection in marketing?

Answer:Choosing the right market based on personal

expertise and audience interest can set the stage for success. You're more likely to thrive if you go deep into a niche rather than spreading too thinly across broad categories.

# 9.Question

Tell me how technology plays a role in list building according to the chapter.

Answer:Technology enables precise targeting and segmentation of lists and can enhance the effectiveness of campaigns through advanced data analytics. However, the author cautions against letting technology dictate marketing strategies rather than ensuring it supports the overarching ideas of the marketing campaign.

# 10.Question

What takeaway does the author suggest regarding listening to the audience?

Answer:It is critical to pay close attention to audience feedback—both verbal and inferred through buying patterns—to tailor offerings and adapt business strategies effectively. Being responsive leads to better alignment with audience needs and increased engagement.

# 11.Question

How should marketers approach communication with their audience segments?

Answer:Marketers should customize communications based on RFM analysis—particularly differentiating between recent buyers, multiple buyers, and top spenders. This allows for targeted messaging that feels personal and relevant, enhancing connection and encouraging further purchases.

# Chapter 5 \| Offers\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What is the essence of creating an irresistible offer?

Answer:An irresistible offer should appeal deeply to your target audience, making it so attractive that only a lunatic would refuse it. To accomplish this, the offer must be aligned with the specific needs, desires, and pain points of the people you’re marketing to.

# 2.Question

Why is niche selection important in creating offers?

Answer:Choosing a niche enables you to focus on a specific segment of the market where you possess expertise and understanding. It allows you to deliver tailored offers that resonate more profoundly with your audience, leading to higher engagement and conversion rates.

# 3.Question

How can understanding why a customer should listen to you enhance your offer's effectiveness?

Answer:Establishing authority and credibility is crucial; you need to answer the question of 'Why should I listen to you?' by drawing on your experiences and expertise. This makes your offer more compelling, as customers feel assured they are engaging with someone knowledgeable.

# 4.Question

What role does testing play in refining an offer?

Answer:Testing, particularly through methods like the Q test, provides quantitative data that informs you about what your audience is truly interested in. This data helps ensure that the offers you present are desirable and thus more likely to yield

positive responses.

# 5.Question

How do you balance innovation with the success of established offers?

Answer:While it’s critical to maximize successful offers, don’t become overly attached to them. Be willing to innovate and evolve your offers to adapt to changing audience interests and market conditions.

# 6.Question

Why should you prioritize your existing assets in marketing?

Answer:Before seeking out new ideas or assets, assess what you already have—your existing lists, content, and promotions can often be repurposed effectively. This focus on existing assets allows you to leverage previous successes for future growth.

# 7.Question

In what ways do premiums enhance an offer?

Answer:Bonuses and premiums can significantly increase the value perception of an offer. They can transform a good offer

into a great one by providing additional incentives that encourage engagement and purchasing.

# 8.Question

What does it mean that offers are creative?

Answer:The creation of compelling offers involves creativity, as it requires thinking outside the box to find unique elements that will genuinely appeal to your audience. It's crucial to ensure that your offers are not only effective but also innovative.

# 9.Question

How can measuring the performance of offers improve future marketing efforts?

Answer:Lasting success comes from consistently analyzing the performance of your offers. This data-driven approach allows you to identify what works, enabling you to refine your strategies and continually improve over time.

# 10.Question

What is the significance of the phrase 'curiosity + optimism + measurement'?

Answer:This phrase encapsulates the formula for successful

direct marketing: being curious about what engages customers, optimistic in testing ideas, and disciplined in measuring results to ensure offers are effective.

# Chapter 6 \| Creative & Copy\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What is the key to successful marketing campaigns according to Chapter 6?

Answer:The key to successful marketing campaigns is the combination of having a thoroughly targeted list, an irresistible offer, and exceptional creative copy. When these three elements are perfectly aligned, they create long-lasting and sustainable business success, rather than just quick wins.

# 2.Question

Why is understanding your audience crucial for copywriters?

Answer:Understanding the audience is crucial because it allows copywriters to create messages that resonate deeply with the readers. The best copywriters obsess over their

audience’s behavior, interests, and language to craft compelling and effective copy that motivates action.

# 3.Question

What is the significance of the '41/39/20 rule' mentioned in Chapter 6?

Answer:The '41/39/20 rule' emphasizes that the success of a marketing campaign is derived from $4 1 %$ list selection, $3 9 %$ the offer, and $2 0 %$ the creative copy. This highlights that while creative is important, it should not be prioritized over a well-selected list and a strong offer.

# 4.Question

How does 'humility' play a role in a successful copywriter's career?

Answer:Humility allows copywriters to learn from their failures and seek continuous improvement. Successful copywriters recognize their limitations and are not afraid to ask for help or feedback from others, which ultimately leads to better results.

# 5.Question

What are the seven characteristics of a world-class

# copywriter?

Answer:The seven characteristics are: 1) Hunger (dedication to the craft), 2) Curiosity (insatiable desire to learn about the audience), 3) Smarts (surrounding themselves with talented peers), 4) Passion (deep interest in subjects), 5)

Understanding direct marketing principles (being an advisor),

6. Humility (willingness to learn from failures), and 7) How they share their success (generosity in sharing knowledge and achievements).

# 6.Question

What does it mean that copywriters are like 'canaries in the coal mine'?

Answer:This metaphor suggests that great copywriters are often the first to detect changes and trends in the market. Their deep research and understanding of audience psychology allow them to foresee what will resonate with consumers before others do.

# 7.Question

Why is 'passion' important for a copywriter according to

# Chapter 6?

Answer:Passion is important because it drives copywriters to write about topics that excite them, leading to more authentic and compelling content. When a copywriter is passionate about what they are writing, it translates into higher quality copy that engages and motivates readers.

# 8.Question

How should marketers approach the hiring of copywriters?

Answer:Marketers should approach hiring by looking for copywriters who exhibit the seven key characteristics discussed. They should seek candidates who truly understand the direct marketing principles and demonstrate a willingness to dive deep into understanding the target audience.

# 9.Question

What lesson can be learned from the relationship between message and audience in marketing?

Answer:The relationship between message and audience is paramount; successful marketing requires tailoring messages

to align with the audience's language, concerns, and motivations. This ensures better engagement and higher conversion rates.

# 10.Question

How can marketers benefit from collaborating closely with copywriters?

Answer:By collaborating closely, marketers can share their vision and insights with copywriters, enabling them to create more impactful and effective messaging. This partnership maximizes the potential for successful campaigns that resonate with the target market.

# Chapter 7 \| Multichannel Marketing\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What is the main caution that Brian Kurtz emphasizes about relying on a single marketing channel?

Answer:Relying on a single marketing channel can be dangerous because it risks the long-term viability of your business. If that channel fails or changes unpredictably, your entire business could suffer. It's critical to diversify your marketing channels to protect against unforeseen events and ensure continued access to profitable customers.

# 2.Question

How does Brian Kurtz differentiate between the value of large audiences and targeted audiences?

Answer:Kurtz argues that smaller, more targeted audiences often provide higher lifetime value (LTV) than larger audiences. Quality over quantity is key; it's better to have fewer customers who are highly engaged and willing to make larger purchases than a vast number of unresponsive

# 3.Question

What does Brian Kurtz mean by 'never define yourself by a channel'? Why is this perspective important?

Answer:Brian Kurtz suggests that marketers should not limit themselves by identifying strictly with one marketing channel (like social media or e-mail). This perspective is crucial because it keeps marketers open to exploring a variety of channels that might be more effective for their products, fostering adaptability and innovation.

# 4.Question

What example does Kurtz provide to illustrate the risk of one-channel dependency?

Answer:Kurtz shares a story of an online business that vanished overnight when it was cut off from a single e-commerce platform it depended on. This highlights the importance of diversifying marketing efforts across multiple channels to avoid being severely impacted by changes in any one channel.

# 5.Question

What strategy does Kurtz recommend for understanding customers and determining the best marketing channels to use?

Answer:Kurtz recommends conducting thorough research into your target audience, including their pain points, where they discuss these issues, their demographic and psychographic profiles, and what channels they frequent. Understanding these aspects helps identify the most effective marketing strategies and channels to reach them.

# 6.Question

How does Kurtz emphasize the importance of metrics in multichannel marketing?

Answer:Kurtz emphasizes that metrics are fundamental to multichannel marketing. He insists on tracking key performance indicators like lifetime value (LTV), cost to acquire customers, and attribution, which help assess the profitability and effectiveness of different channels and campaigns.

# 7.Question

What does Kurtz mean by the term 'attribution' in marketing, and why is it significant?

Answer:Attribution in marketing refers to tracking a customer's journey and interactions across various channels before they make a purchase. It's significant because it helps marketers understand which channels are most effective, allowing them to allocate resources efficiently and maximize profitability.

# 8.Question

Can you explain the 'online to offline to online' (O to O to O) concept that Kurtz discusses?

Answer:The 'O to O to O' concept refers to the practice of guiding customers through various interactions across online and offline channels. For instance, a customer may first encounter a product through online advertising, then receive a direct mail piece, and finally make a purchase online. This approach leverages different media to enhance customer engagement and lifetime value.

# 9.Question

# What is Kurtz's stance on hiring agencies or specialists for marketing?

Answer:Kurtz advocates for hiring specialists for each marketing channel instead of relying on full-service agencies. He believes that no one can excel in every aspect of marketing, so seeking out experts ensures that each channel is maximized effectively.

# 10.Question

Why is understanding customer lifetime value (LTV) crucial according to Kurtz?

Answer:Understanding LTV is crucial because it informs marketers how much they can afford to spend on acquiring customers. It helps in assessing the long-term profitability of customers, justifying marketing expenditures, and designing appropriate offers that encourage repeat business.

# Chapter 8 \| Customer Service & Fulfillment\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What lesson can we learn from umpiring when it comes to customer service?

Answer:Umpiring, like customer service, is about creating order from chaos and fulfilling the needs of the experience without expecting recognition. Just as an umpire strives to make the game enjoyable for players and fans, customer service should focus on ensuring the customer's experience is seamless and satisfactory.

# 2.Question

Why is it crucial to overdeliver in customer service?

Answer:Overdelivering in customer service is essential because it transforms a customer's negative experience into a positive one, fostering loyalty. When customers feel valued and receive more than what they expect, they are more likely to return and recommend your services, increasing their lifetime value.

# 3.Question

How can we prevent losing customers after a service failure?

Answer:To prevent losing customers after a service failure, respond promptly and generously to their concerns.

Acknowledge their situation, offer bonuses or extra value, and strive to exceed their expectations in resolution to turn a negative experience into one that strengthens the customer relationship.

# 4.Question

What is the importance of listening to customer feedback?

Answer:Listening to customer feedback is critical as it provides invaluable insights that can reveal potential issues within your service or product. Direct interactions with customers often uncover problems that data alone does not reveal, allowing for proactive improvements.

# 5.Question

What does the adage 'it's easier to keep a customer than to get a new one' signify?

Answer:This adage underscores the value of nurturing existing relationships. Retaining customers typically involves lower costs and efforts compared to acquiring new ones, as

satisfied customers tend to return and offer referrals.

# 6.Question

How does identifying as 'customer-centric' benefit a business overall?

Answer:Identifying as 'customer-centric' cultivates loyalty and enhances the overall customer experience, leading to longer-term engagements. Companies that prioritize customer service create strong barriers to switching, as customers feel deeply valued and understood.

# 7.Question

What role do secret shoppers play in improving customer service?

Answer:Secret shoppers provide firsthand observations about the customer experience, asking crucial questions and identifying weaknesses in service or operations that may go unnoticed. Their feedback is instrumental in enhancing service quality and accountability.

# 8.Question

What is a 'barrier to switch' and why is it important?

Answer:A 'barrier to switch' refers to the strategies implemented by businesses to make it difficult for customers to leave due to superior service or uniquely tailored experiences. It is important because it fosters greater loyalty and customer retention against competitors.

# 9.Question

How can businesses effectively mitigate predictable distractions in their marketing?

Answer:Businesses can mitigate predictable distractions in marketing by planning their campaigns around significant events and ensuring their promotions are timed when the audience is most receptive. This strategic planning helps maintain customer engagement and response rates.

# 10.Question

What should a company prioritize to ensure exceptional customer service?

Answer:A company should prioritize hiring and retaining skilled customer service individuals, fostering a culture of overdelivery and responsiveness, and constantly seeking to understand and improve the customer experience.

# Chapter 9 \| Continuity & LTV\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What is the primary importance of establishing a suite of products in a direct marketing business?

Answer:Developing multiple products, rather than focusing solely on one, creates a foundation for repeat business and customer loyalty, ensuring long-term success.

# 2.Question

How does understanding Lifetime Value (LTV) change the way you approach marketing strategies?

Answer:Understanding LTV allows businesses to take a long-term perspective on marketing investments, justifying initial losses on customer acquisition with the expectation of higher returns over time.

# 3.Question

What is the 'bogey' in the context of marketing, and why is it crucial?

Answer:The bogey is the calculated amount you are willing to lose on initial customer acquisition, based on expected

LTV. It is crucial because it guides marketing spending and helps assess the profitability of each channel.

# 4.Question

What are the dangers of not tracking the lifetime value of customers?

Answer:Failing to track LTV can lead to prematurely abandoning profitable channels, misattributing success to luck, or overestimating the profitability of channels, which can all undermine the business's growth.

# 5.Question

Why is congruency important in marketing, and what can be the consequences of a congruence fail?

Answer:Congruency ensures that marketing aligns with the company's values and customer expectations; failing to maintain this can lead to customer distrust, unsubscribes, and damage to long-term business relationships.

# 6.Question

How can businesses utilize insights from LTV to improve their marketing spend?

Answer:By accurately calculating LTV, businesses can justify

spending more on customer acquisition, knowing exactly when they will start achieving profitability from those investments.

# 7.Question

What insights can be drawn from the author's experience with the list rental incident?

Answer:The incident highlights the importance of maintaining customer trust and congruency in offers; short-term financial gain should never compromise the long-term relationship with your audience.

# 8.Question

How does the author suggest balancing aggressive marketing with a sustainable business model?

Answer:By calculating the bogey for different products and maintaining cash flow discipline, businesses can be aggressive in marketing while ensuring they do not overextend financially.

# 9.Question

What role does editorial quality play in customer retention and LTV?

Answer:High editorial quality increases customer satisfaction, leading to higher renewal rates and additional purchases, which significantly boosts LTV.

# 10.Question

What is the takeaway regarding spending on marketing based on LTV?

Answer:Businesses should feel empowered to spend more on up-front marketing if they have a solid understanding of LTV, as it allows for informed risk-taking and investment strategies.

# Chapter 10 \| Playing the Long Game\| Q&A

# 1.Question

What is the main shift in mindset that the author experienced regarding networking?

Answer:The author shifted from viewing networking as superficial "schmoozing" to understanding it as a process of contributing to others first in order to create genuine connections.

# 2.Question

How can contributing to connect benefit your career and relationships?

Answer:By consistently contributing to others without expecting immediate returns, you build meaningful relationships over time that can lead to partnerships, opportunities, and mutual growth.

# 3.Question

What lesson did the author learn about the importance of face-to-face connection?

Answer:Face-to-face connection is more powerful than

digital interactions; true relationships thrive on personal

engagement rather than superficial online contacts.

# 4.Question

How does the concept of relationship capital work, and why is it valuable?

Answer:Relationship capital is the value derived from your personal and professional relationships. It accumulates over time and offers greater long-term benefits than just financial returns.

# 5.Question

What are intentional dinners, and why are they effective for building connections?

Answer:Intentional dinners are carefully planned gatherings that bring together interesting people in a conducive environment to foster deep conversations and meaningful connections.

# 6.Question

How can one differentiate themselves in building relationships according to the author?

Answer:By consistently adding a personal touch, such as sending handwritten notes or thoughtful, personalized gifts,

rather than just following conventional practices.

# 7.Question

What is the significance of humility in networking and relationship building?

Answer:Humility allows you to connect with all individuals regardless of their status, recognizing that everyone has a unique contribution to make and fostering more genuine interactions.

# 8.Question

What does the author suggest about the practice of sending holiday cards?

Answer:The author suggests that sending genuine, thoughtful messages throughout the year can be more impactful than just holiday cards, as it shows continuous care and attention.

# 9.Question

What is the ultimate takeaway from the chapter regarding success and relationships?

Answer:Success is not solely based on numbers but on the quality of relationships you cultivate; those connections enrich your life and business.

# 10.Question

Why does the author emphasize the importance of being 'interested' rather than 'interesting' during interactions? Answer:Focusing on being interested in others builds deeper connections and shows genuine care, which ultimately strengthens your network.

# Brian Kurtz: How to Build a Business Empire with Direct Marketing

![EP30 Thumbnail](https://www.radical-marketing.com/wp-content/uploads/elementor/thumbs/EP30-Thumbnail-qqw3rjzgc3n3ayysl7czetk6tmepip3pz0uh8bhjm0.jpg)

- [By\\
Terry Tam](https://www.radical-marketing.com/author/bookpal/)
- [July 15, 2024](https://www.radical-marketing.com/2024/07/15/)
- 12:33 pm
- [No Comments](https://www.radical-marketing.com/brian-kurtz-how-to-build-a-business-empire-with-direct-marketing/#respond)

Imagine being so allergic to selling that people dub you the ‘Director of Sales Prevention’.

But for Brian Kurtz, an OG marketer who’s worked with legends like Gene Schwartz, Dan Kennedy, Gary Bencivenga?

It’s actually one of the many secrets behind his WILDLY successful direct marketing career.

He’s finally on the show, so today you’ll learn:

- The HUGE mistake many marketers make with their copy
- How to find a mentor (and make them want to guide you)
- Why selling shouldn’t be your priority if you want your business to last
- … and other ways to take your business to the next level

If you’re in it for the long haul, then Brian’s tips are pure gold.

#### Full Interview:

Why Marketers Need A Director of Sales Prevention Like Brian Kurtz \| Radical Marketing - YouTube

Tap to unmute

[Why Marketers Need A Director of Sales Prevention Like Brian Kurtz \| Radical Marketing](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tEjOyWKw3J0) [Terence Tam](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCIB4P2AZeVChl_N1DdFQgnA)

![thumbnail-image](https://yt3.ggpht.com/xbjEeAX02NSR1ObhegrbZFktQbqvRgJgGt2QNEsOzh-kKZgj-B-2YkoIKJjql__x9tnown4c=s68-c-k-c0x00ffffff-no-rj)

- 59:21 – Personal Development and Marketing Intertwined

>
> \- Brian Kurtz - [Tweet](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=%22I+innovated+on+the+40-40-20+rule+to+prove+that+the+list+is+the+most+important%2C+your+audience%2C+the+media+you%27re+buying.+If+you%27re+buying+the+wrong+media%2C+and+you+have+the+best+offer+and+the+best+creative%2C+you+get+zero+orders%2C+but+the+reverse+is+not+true.%22+%E2%80%94+-+Brian+Kurtz+-&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.radical-marketing.com%2Fbrian-kurtz-how-to-build-a-business-empire-with-direct-marketing%2F&via=goradicalmktg)

#### Full Transcript

Brian Kurtz: \[00:00:00\] The audience is everything. I mean, I learned that from Gene Schwartz. He says that copywriters and marketers don’t create desire. The desire is already in the marketplace. You have to like hone it and find it.

Terence: And hi, welcome to Founders Go Naked. Today, I have a very special guest who I’ve been trying to get on this podcast for like months, and finally he’s here. And I’m talking about the great Brian Kurtz. So Brian, welcome to the stage.

Brian Kurtz: Thank you, Terence. It’s been, uh, I’ve been traveling a lot. I haven’t been avoiding you, I swear. Um, you know, you’re one of my favorite people. I know you just wrote a book and I think I, I endorsed it. And I, I love what you do.

Brian Kurtz: You’ve been in my Titans Xcelerator Mastermind, I think almost from the beginning, uh…

Terence: Yeah, almost from the beginning.

Brian Kurtz: Given where you live and where I live, you know, it’s not as convenient and yet you stayed a member for so long. And I admire the loyalty and the support, and I really appreciate you.

Terence: Thanks, Brian. So \[00:01:00\] Brian, I mean, yeah, that, that we’ve been there for like five years and I think I took a little three or four month, uh, vacation in between.

Terence: Right, right.

Brian Kurtz: Yeah.

Terence: Otherwise I’ve been part of the group. So for those of you who don’t know Brian Kurtz, uh, Brian has been in marketing for over 40 years. He has been in Boardroom. He was one of the business builders there, and he’s worked with some of the biggest names in copywriting and marketing history, those that today’s marketers will deem to be legends.

Terence: Or they don’t know who they are. Or they don’t know who they are.

Brian Kurtz: Or they don’t know who they are, right.

Terence: So Brian, name me some of those legends that you’ve worked with.

Brian Kurtz: Well, going back, you know, obviously my ultimate mentor was the guy who founded Boardroom, Marty Edelston, and he might not be known unless you’re on my…in my online family and you read my blog every week, you wouldn’t probably know who Marty is. Amazing entrepreneur, amazing mentor, teacher. And he taught by showing and not telling, which was always the \[00:02:00\] best, right? You know, it’s an excuse in a way, cause he never told me what to do. He just, he did his thing and I had to like emulate what he was doing.

Brian Kurtz: Um, those, those mentors are great. But then Gene Schwartz, the author of Breakthrough Advertising, some people think that he never existed, but he’s a ghost or something. And, and copywriters and marketers today know all about Gene because I keep his name alive by republishing Breakthrough Advertising, which was written in 1966.

Brian Kurtz: I was eight years old at the time. So I wasn’t even, you know, I’m, there are people that are older than me. Right. Um, but Gene Schwartz and then in the copywriting world, um, Gary Bencivenga and Jim Rutz and… you know, just amazing…. um, uh, legends of copywriting. And I, I got, I’m not gonna say I got lucky, but being with Marty, you know, Marty at Boardroom ran what I call a learning organization.

Brian Kurtz: So he always went to the outside for the best \[00:03:00\] brains.
